title: Identifying outliers
description: Practice identifying outliers using the 1.5*IQR rule.
topic_path: Topic B: Lesson 7: Measuring variability for skewed distributions (interquartile range)

assessment_items.question_content:
The average lifespans of $18$ common species of turtles are shown below along with the five-number summary.

![A stem and leaf plot with stems numbered from 1 to 13 in increments of 1 and with 18 leaves. Stem 1. Leaves 3, 8, 9. Stem 2. Leaves 4, 4, 4, 5, 8. Stem 3. Leaves 0, 0, 0, 7, 8. Stem 4. Leaves 0, 0, 7. Stem 7. Leaves 5, 7.  Stem 13. Leaf 8.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d6c994cd5b6dc8bea3ae3615c97ac70e0ff80957)

Key: $1~|~8$ represents a species of turtle whose average lifespan is $18$ years.

|| **Five-number summary** ||
min | $\text{Q}_1$ | median | $\text{Q}_3$ | max
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$13$ | $24$ | $30$ | $40$ | $138$

**According to the $1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ rule for outliers, how many high outliers are there in the data set?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The $1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ rule for outliers says that any data point greater than $\text{Q}_3+1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ is a high outlier.

Remember that $\text{IQR}=\text{Q}_3-\text{Q}_1$

The five-number summary tells us that $\text{Q}_1=24$ and $\text{Q}_3=40.$ 

$\begin{align} &\phantom{=} \text{Q}_3+1.5\cdot \text{IQR} \\
\\
&=40+1.5\cdot(40-24) \\
\\
&=40+1.5\cdot16 \\
\\
&=40+24 \\
\\
&=64\end{align}$

Any data point greater than $64$ is a high outlier.
There are $3$ high outliers in the data set.

assessment_items.question_content:
The average lifespans of $18$ common species of turtles are shown below along with the five-number summary.

![A stem and leaf plot with stems numbered from 1 to 13 in increments of 1 and with 18 leaves. Stem 1. Leaves 3, 8, 9. Stem 2. Leaves 4, 4, 4, 5, 8. Stem 3. Leaves 0, 0, 0, 7, 8. Stem 4. Leaves 0, 0, 7. Stem 7. Leaves 5, 7.  Stem 13. Leaf 8.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d6c994cd5b6dc8bea3ae3615c97ac70e0ff80957)

Key: $1~|~8$ represents a species of turtle whose average lifespan is $18$ years.

|| **Five-number summary** ||
min | $\text{Q}_1$ | median | $\text{Q}_3$ | max
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$13$ | $24$ | $30$ | $40$ | $138$

**According to the $1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ rule for outliers, how many low outliers are there in the data set?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The $1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ rule for outliers says that any data point less than $\text{Q}_1-1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ is a low outlier.

Remember that $\text{IQR}=\text{Q}_3-\text{Q}_1$

The five-number summary tells us that $\text{Q}_1=24$ and $\text{Q}_3=40$.

$\begin{align} &\phantom{=} \text{Q}_1-1.5\cdot \text{IQR} \\
\\
&=24-1.5\cdot(40-24) \\
\\
&=24-1.5\cdot 16 \\
\\
&=24-24 \\
\\
&=0\end{align}$

Any data point less than $0$ is a low outlier, and there aren't any data points less than $0$.
There are $0$ low outliers in the data set.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following dotplot shows the number of songs on each album in Sal's collection. Each dot represents a different album.

![A dot plot with an axis labeled "number of songs" and numbered from 0 to 20 in increments of 1, with 19 points. 4, frequency 1. 7, frequency 1. 8, frequency 2. 9, frequency 2. 10, frequency 4. 11, frequency 1. 12, frequency 2. 13, frequency 2. 14, frequency 1. 16, frequency 1. 18, frequency 1. 20, frequency 1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/dcf90c663ea568371538c6c9ef22f273076b2c62)

Here is the five-number summary for these data:

|| **Five-number summary** ||
min | $\text{Q}_1$ | median | $\text{Q}_3$ | max
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$4$ | $9$ | $10$ | $13$ | $20$

**According to the $1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ rule for outliers, how many high outliers are there in the data set?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The $1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ rule for outliers says that any data point greater than $\text{Q}_3+1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ is a high outlier.

Remember that $\text{IQR}=\text{Q}_3-\text{Q}_1$

The five-number summary tells us that $\text{Q}_1=9$ and $\text{Q}_3=13.$ 

$\begin{align} &\phantom{=} \text{Q}_3+1.5\cdot \text{IQR} \\
\\
&=13+1.5\cdot(13-9) \\
\\
&=13+1.5\cdot4 \\
\\
&=13+6 \\
\\
&=19\end{align}$

Any data point greater than $19$ is a high outlier.

There is $1$ data point greater than $19$.

![The same dot plot as before, with a line separating points left and right of 19.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/fb601dbcad514ec5d7a0c93db70c66d87568a837)
There is $1$ high outlier in the data set.

![A dot plot has a horizontal axis labeled, Number of songs, marked from 0 to 20, in increments of 1. Here is the five-number summary for these data:

|| **Five-number summary** ||
min | $\text{Q}_1$ | median | $\text{Q}_3$ | max
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$4$ | $9$ | $10$ | $13$ | $20$

**According to the $1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ rule for outliers, how many low outliers are there in the data set?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The $1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ rule for outliers says that any data point less than $\text{Q}_1-1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ is a low outlier.

Remember that $\text{IQR}=\text{Q}_3-\text{Q}_1$

The five-number summary tells us that $\text{Q}_1=9$ and $\text{Q}_3=13.$ 

$\begin{align} &\phantom{=} \text{Q}_1-1.5\cdot \text{IQR} \\
\\
&=9-1.5\cdot(13-9) \\
\\
&=9-1.5\cdot4 \\
\\
&=9-6 \\
\\
&=3\end{align}$

Any data point less than $3$ is a low outlier.

There are no data points less than $3$.

![A dot plot has a horizontal axis labeled, Number of songs, marked from 0 to 20, in increments of 1. The number of dots above each value is as follows: 4, 1; 7, 1; 8, 2; 9, 2; 10, 4; 11, 1; 12, 2; 13, 2; 14, 1; 16, 1; 18, 1; 20, 1. There are $0$ low outliers in the data set.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following dotplot shows the number of e-mails that Alejandro deleted each day. Each dot represents a different day.

![A dot plot with an axis labeled "number of e-mails" and numbered from 0 to 10 in increments of 1, with 20 points. 1, frequency 1. 2, frequency 7. 3, frequency 5. 5, frequency 3. 6, frequency 2. 7, frequency 1. 9, frequency 1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/fb801de708b0b8696b11a2c002325d20433666e8)

Here is the five-number summary for these data:

|| **Five-number summary** ||
min | $\text{Q}_1$ | median | $\text{Q}_3$ | max
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$1$ | $2$ | $3$ | $5$ | $9$

**According to the $1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ rule for outliers, how many high outliers are there in the data set?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The $1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ rule for outliers says that any data point greater than $\text{Q}_3+1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ is a high outlier.

Remember that $\text{IQR}=\text{Q}_3-\text{Q}_1$

The five-number summary tells us that $\text{Q}_1=2$ and $\text{Q}_3=5.$ 

$\begin{align} &\phantom{=} \text{Q}_3+1.5\cdot \text{IQR} \\
\\
&=5+1.5\cdot(5-2) \\
\\
&=5+1.5\cdot 3 \\
\\
&=5 + 4.5 \\
\\
&=9.5\end{align}$

Any data point greater than $9.5$ is a high outlier.

There are $0$ data points greater than $9.5$.

![The same dot plot as before, with a line separating the axis left and right of 9.5.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8cbf41df2a9f60d2c3ec00019f5c70d0f4699055)
There are $0$ high outliers in the data set.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following dotplot shows the number of e-mails that Alejandro deleted each day. Each dot represents a different day.

![A dot plot with an axis labeled "number of e-mails" and numbered from 0 to 10 in increments of 1, with 20 points. 1, frequency 1. 2, frequency 7. 3, frequency 5. 5, frequency 3. 6, frequency 2. 7, frequency 1. 9, frequency 1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/fb801de708b0b8696b11a2c002325d20433666e8)

Here is the five-number summary for these data:

|| **Five-number summary** ||
min | $\text{Q}_1$ | median | $\text{Q}_3$ | max
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$1$ | $2$ | $3$ | $5$ | $9$

**According to the $1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ rule for outliers, how many low outliers are there in the data set?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The $1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ rule for outliers says that any data point less than $\text{Q}_1-1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ is a low outlier.

Remember that $\text{IQR}=\text{Q}_3-\text{Q}_1$

The five-number summary tells us that $\text{Q}_1=2$ and $\text{Q}_3=5$.

$\begin{align} &\phantom{=} \text{Q}_1-1.5\cdot \text{IQR} \\
\\
&=2-1.5\cdot(5-2) \\
\\
&=2-1.5\cdot3 \\
\\
&=2-4.5 \\
\\
&=-2.5\end{align}$

Any data point less than $-2.5$ is a low outlier.
There are $0$ data points less than $-2.5$ because a negative value does not make sense in this context.
There are $0$ low outliers in the data set.

assessment_items.question_content:
The stem and leaf plot shows the number of items in each of the $12$ collections in a museum.

![A stem and leaf plot provides the number of items in each of 12 collections in a museum ranging from 3 to 39. A key indicates a stem of 2 with a leaf of 6 represents a collection with 26 items in it. Data in the chart is as follows. Stem 0; Leaf, 3, 3, 5, 7, 9. Stem 1; Leaf, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3. Stem 2; Leaf, 6. Stem 3; Leaf, 9.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/09465775afd0a50e074bf3b35e3c376dbc60d272)

Key: $2~|~6$ represents a collection with $26$ items in it.

Here is the five-number summary for these data:

|| **Five-number summary** ||
min | $\text{Q}_1$ | median | $\text{Q}_3$ | max
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$3$ | $6$ | $12$ | $13$ | $39$

**According to the $1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ rule for outliers, how many high outliers are there in the data set?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[https://www.google.com/culturalinstitute/beta/u/0/partner/museo-reina-sofia]: (source)
hints:
The $1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ rule for outliers says that any data point greater than $\text{Q}_3+1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ is a high outlier.

Remember that $\text{IQR}=\text{Q}_3-\text{Q}_1$

The five-number summary tells us that $\text{Q}_1=6$ and $\text{Q}_3=13$.

$\begin{align} &\phantom{=} \text{Q}_3+1.5\cdot \text{IQR} \\
\\
&=13+1.5\cdot(13-6) \\
\\
&=13+1.5\cdot 7 \\
\\
&=13 + 10.5 \\
\\
&=23.5\end{align}$

Any data point greater than $23.5$ is a high outlier.

There are $2$ data points—$26$ and $39$—greater than $23.5$.
There are $2$ high outliers in the data set.

hints:

assessment_items.question_content:
The stem and leaf plot shows the number of days of snowfall at a winter sports resort for each of the past $17$ years.

![A stem and leaf plot provides the number of days of snowfall ranging from 50 to 79. A key indicates a stem of 6 with a leaf of 2 represents a year with 62 days of snowfall. Data in the chart is as follows. Stem 5; Leaf, 0, 7. Stem 6; Leaf, 2, 4, 5, 5, 5, 8. Stem 7; Leaf, 0, 1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 7, 9, 9.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ce2a9aea5d88b3650bec78ab73641488b9653313)

Key: $6~|~2$ represents a year with $62$ days of snowfall.

Here is the five-number summary for these data:

|| **Five-number summary** ||
min | $\text{Q}_1$ | median | $\text{Q}_3$ | max
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$50$ | $64.5$ | $70$ | $73.5$ | $79$

**According to the $1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ rule for outliers, how many high outliers are there in the data set?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[http://www.onthesnow.com/utah/snowbird/historical-snowfall.html]: (source)
hints:
The $1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ rule for outliers says that any data point greater than $\text{Q}_3+1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ is a high outlier.

Remember that $\text{IQR}=\text{Q}_3-\text{Q}_1$

The five-number summary tells us that $\text{Q}_1=64.5$ and $\text{Q}_3=73.5$.

$\begin{align} &\phantom{=} \text{Q}_3+1.5\cdot \text{IQR} \\
\\
&=73.5+1.5\cdot(73.5-64.5) \\
\\
&=73.5+1.5\cdot 9 \\
\\
&=73.5 + 13.5 \\
\\
&=87\end{align}$

Any data point greater than $87$ is a high outlier, and there aren't any data points greater than $87$.
There are $0$ high outliers in the data set.

**According to the $1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ rule for outliers, how many low outliers are there in the data set?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[http://www.onthesnow.com/utah/snowbird/historical-snowfall.html]: (source)
hints:
The $1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ rule for outliers says that any data point less than $\text{Q}_1-1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ is a low outlier.

Remember that $\text{IQR}=\text{Q}_3-\text{Q}_1$

The five-number summary tells us that $\text{Q}_1=64.5$ and $\text{Q}_3=73.5$.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following dotplot shows the centuries during which the $11$ castles whose ruins remain in Somerset, England were constructed. Each dot represents a different castle.

![A dot plot has a horizontal axis labeled, Century of construction, marked from 10 to 20 in increments of 1. The number of dots above each value is as follows: 12, 1; 13, 3; 14, 4; 17, 2; 19, 1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2dd50107a61b2c3ccaa130540f1757f5e7c6859e)

Here is the five-number summary for these data:

|| **Five-number summary** ||
min | $\text{Q}_1$ | median | $\text{Q}_3$ | max
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$12$ | $13$ | $14$ | $17$ | $19$

**According to the $1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ rule for outliers, how many high outliers are there in the data set?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_castles_in_England]: (Source)
hints:
The $1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ rule for outliers says that any data point greater than $\text{Q}_3+1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ is a high outlier.

Remember that $\text{IQR}=\text{Q}_3-\text{Q}_1$

The five-number summary tells us that $\text{Q}_1=13$ and $\text{Q}_3=17$.

$\begin{align} &\phantom{=} \text{Q}_3+1.5\cdot \text{IQR} \\
\\
&=17+1.5\cdot(17-13) \\
\\
&=17+1.5\cdot 4 \\
\\
&=17 + 6 \\
\\
&=23\end{align}$

Any data point greater than $23$ is a high outlier, and there aren't any data points greater than $23$.
There are $0$ high outliers in the data set.

**According to the $1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ rule for outliers, how many low outliers are there in the data set?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_castles_in_England]: (Source)
hints:
The $1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ rule for outliers says that any data point less than $\text{Q}_1-1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ is a low outlier.

Remember that $\text{IQR}=\text{Q}_3-\text{Q}_1$

The five-number summary tells us that $\text{Q}_1=13$ and $\text{Q}_3=17$.

$\begin{align} &\phantom{=} \text{Q}_1-1.5\cdot \text{IQR} \\
\\
&=13-1.5\cdot(17-13) \\
\\
&=13-1.5\cdot 4 \\
\\
&=13-6 \\
\\
&=7\end{align}$

Any data point less than $7$ is a low outlier, and there aren't any data points less than $7$.
There are $0$ low outliers in the data set.

assessment_items.question_content:
Zip codes are numerical designations of locations for the purpose of mail delivery. The following histogram summarizes the number, in hundreds, of zip codes assigned to each state in the United States.

![A histogram plots Number of states versus Number of zip codes, in hundreds, from 0 to 24 and over, in increments of 2. There are no bars between 12 and 14, 16 and 20, and between 22 and 24. Bar heights are approximately as follows. From 0 to 2, 12. From 2 to 4, 6. From 4 to 6, 8. From 6 to 8, 9. From 8 to 10, 5. From 10 to 12, 4. From 14 to 16, 3. From 20 to 22, 2. 24 and over, 1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a7e112b4c2b3d59d7ee60f536b29559db2d77c19)

Here is the five-number summary for these data:

|| **Five-number summary** ||
min | $\text{Q}_1$ | median | $\text{Q}_3$ | max
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$1$ | $275$ | $533$ | $933$ | $2596$

**According to the $1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ rule for outliers, how many high outliers are there in the data set?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[https://www.zip-codes.com/zip-code-statistics.asp]: (Source)
hints:
The $1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ rule for outliers says that any data point greater than $\text{Q}_3+1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ is a high outlier.

Remember that $\text{IQR}=\text{Q}_3-\text{Q}_1$

The five-number summary tells us that $\text{Q}_1=275$ and $\text{Q}_3=933$.

$\begin{align} &\phantom{=} \text{Q}_3+1.5\cdot \text{IQR} \\
\\
&=933+1.5\cdot(933-275) \\
\\
&=933+1.5\cdot 658 \\
\\
&=933 + 987 \\
\\
&=1920\end{align}$

Any data point greater than $1920$ is a high outlier.

There are $2+1=3$ data points greater than $1920$.

![A histogram plots Number of states versus Number of zip codes, in hundreds, from 0 to 24 and over, in increments of 2. There are no bars between 12 and 14, 16 and 20, and between 22 and 24. Bar heights are approximately as follows. From 0 to 2, 12. From 2 to 4, 6. From 4 to 6, 8. From 6 to 8, 9. From 8 to 10, 5. From 10 to 12, 4. From 14 to 16, 3. From 20 to 22, 2. 24 and over, 1. A dashed vertical line rises through the horizontal axis at approximately 19.20.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/22028cfc2d41b1a26a7a1a4b4c4a47d6109a8695)
There are $3$ high outliers in the data set.

hints:
The $1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ rule for outliers says that any data point less than $\text{Q}_1-1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ is a low outlier.

Remember that $\text{IQR}=\text{Q}_3-\text{Q}_1$

The five-number summary tells us that $\text{Q}_1=275$ and $\text{Q}_3=933$.

$\begin{align} &\phantom{=} \text{Q}_1-1.5\cdot \text{IQR} \\
\\
&=275-1.5\cdot(933-275) \\
\\
&=275-1.5\cdot 658 \\
\\
&=275-987 \\
\\
&=-712\end{align}$

Any data point less than $-712$ is a low outlier, and there aren't any data points less than $-712$.
There are $0$ low outliers in the data set.

**According to the $1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ rule for outliers, how many high outliers are there in the data set?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[http://www.epi.org/publication/wage-workers-older-88-percent-workers-benefit/]: (Source)
hints:
The $1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ rule for outliers says that any data point greater than $\text{Q}_3+1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ is a high outlier.

Remember that $\text{IQR}=\text{Q}_3-\text{Q}_1$

The five-number summary tells us that $\text{Q}_1=31$ and $\text{Q}_3=42$.

$\begin{align} &\phantom{=} \text{Q}_3+1.5\cdot \text{IQR} \\
\\
&=42+1.5\cdot(42-31) \\
\\
&=42+1.5\cdot 11 \\
\\
&=42 + 16.5 \\
\\
&=58.5\end{align}$

Any data point greater than $58.5$ is a high outlier.

There is $1$ data point greater than $58.5$.

![A histogram plots Number of employees versus Age of employee, from 10 to 60 and over, in increments of 5. There are no bars between 20 and 25 and between 55 and 60. Bar heights are approximately as follows. From 10 to 15, 1. From 15 to 20, 1. From 25 to 30, 4. From 30 to 35, 6. From 35 to 40, 5. From 40 to 45, 5. From 45 to 50, 1. From 50 to 55, 1. 60 and over, 1. A dashed vertical line rises through the horizontal axis at approximately 58.5.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/998e2e68272960f1916dc780210a4cddb5975e7f)
There is $1$ high outlier in the data set.

**According to the $1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ rule for outliers, how many low outliers are there in the data set?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

hints:
The $1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ rule for outliers says that any data point less than $\text{Q}_1-1.5\cdot \text{IQR}$ is a low outlier.

Remember that $\text{IQR}=\text{Q}_3-\text{Q}_1$

The five-number summary tells us that $\text{Q}_1=31$ and $\text{Q}_3=42$.

$\begin{align} &\phantom{=} \text{Q}_1-1.5\cdot \text{IQR} \\
\\
&=31-1.5\cdot(42-31) \\
\\
&=31-1.5\cdot 11 \\
\\
&=31-16.5 \\
\\
&=14.5\end{align}$

Any data point less than $14.5$ is a low outlier.

There is $1$ data point in the bucket $10\leq\text{age}<15$, and the five-number summary we were given confirms that the value of that point is $13$ years old. So there is $1$ data point less than $14.5$.

![A histogram plots Number of employees versus Age of employee, from 10 to 60 and over, in increments of 5. There are no bars between 20 and 25 and between 55 and 60. From 50 to 55, 1. 60 and over, 1. A dashed vertical line rises through the horizontal axis at approximately 14.5.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/39c5aa27e0819a1e7b3c6b2ebeeabedb5e9ec4ed)
There is $1$ low outlier in the data set.
